Q1: I tried to keep a pretty structured routine, honestly. With 32 hours a week dedicated to studying, I'd block out time in the mornings and evenings, usually spreading it across most days. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time going through notes, rewriting key concepts, and summarizing materials. The gamified tasks actually helped me stay on track because they gave me these mini-goals to hit. Even when my anxiety spiked, I'd push through because I knew consistency was the only way I'd make it.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, but I'd be lying if I said deadlines didn't shake things up. I tried to study at the same times each day, but when big assignments were due, I'd shift into overdrive and study way more intensely. My extracurriculars also meant I had to be flexible sometimes. But overall, I preferred routine because it helped manage my anxiety—knowing what to expect each day kept me grounded.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I wanted to check if there were new materials or announcements. The gamified elements were actually a big motivator—I liked seeing my progress and earning those little rewards. Sometimes I'd log in just because I felt anxious about missing something important. With 12 online courses to juggle, I had to stay on top of things or I'd fall behind fast.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became automatic. Every morning with my coffee, I'd open the LMS and check what was new. The gamified task system really helped cement this habit because there was always something to work toward. Once I started seeing my engagement stats go up, it felt rewarding to maintain that streak. It became less of a chore and more like checking social media, honestly.
Q5: The reading materials and written guides were gold for me since I learn best through text. The discussion forums were surprisingly helpful too—I posted quite a bit and reading other students' perspectives helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. The gamified tasks made even boring content feel more engaging because there was a sense of accomplishment attached. Videos were okay, but I preferred having something I could read and annotate.
Q6: Some of the video lectures I'd skim through or play at double speed. I know I watched about 87 minutes total, but honestly, videos aren't my thing—I retain information better when I read it. Some supplementary resources that seemed repetitive I'd skip entirely. If something didn't feel directly tied to assignments or exams, it was harder to justify spending time on it when I was already stretched thin.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to my grades or my understanding of core concepts. The gamified elements helped a lot—when I could see points or badges attached to something, I was more motivated. Also, if an activity sparked genuine curiosity or surprised me with new information, that made it feel valuable. I needed to feel like I was making progress, not just going through motions.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With my anxiety, I'd stress about whether skipping them would hurt me somehow, but I also knew I had to prioritize. Required activities had this weight to them—I'd do them no matter what. Optional ones I'd approach when I felt good, but if I was overwhelmed, they were the first to go. The gamified ones blurred this line though, because even optional tasks had rewards.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a ton of PDFs and readings to study offline. I'd print things out, highlight them, make handwritten notes in the margins. I also used external resources like YouTube explanations and academic articles I found through Google Scholar. A lot of my best studying happened with my notebook open and the computer closed. The LMS was just the starting point for me.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading printed materials, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also discuss concepts with friends from my extracurriculars who were in similar courses. Sometimes I'd work through problems on paper, trying different approaches, and only log back in to submit the final answer. My real learning often happened away from the screen.
Q11: All the time. I'd download lecture notes or readings once, then work with them for days without logging back in. I preferred having physical copies or offline files because I could study anywhere—on the bus, in the library, wherever. It also helped my anxiety to not be constantly connected and seeing notifications. One download could fuel a whole week of studying.
Q12: Midterms were brutal. I had multiple courses demanding attention simultaneously, and my anxiety went through the roof. There were moments where I'd stare at the LMS feeling paralyzed, not knowing where to start. Personal stuff came up too—family obligations and extracurricular commitments that couldn't be ignored. But I pushed through because I've learned that's what I have to do. The resilience I've built got me past those rough patches.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or confused about a topic, I'd log in but not really absorb anything. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars meant some weeks the LMS got less attention than it deserved. Fatigue made me more likely to just check boxes rather than deeply engage. But I tried to recognize when this was happening and come back to materials when I was in a better headspace.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or watch videos while my mind was completely elsewhere. My anxiety sometimes made me feel like I had to be doing something, so I'd log in and go through the motions without really learning. I'd realize later I couldn't remember anything from that session. It was like my body was present but my brain had checked out.
Q15: I procrastinated on some of the longer written assignments, even though I knew they were important. My anxiety would build up around them—the bigger the task, the more overwhelming it felt to start. I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better first, which was sometimes true but sometimes just an excuse. Eventually deadline pressure would force my hand, and I'd get it done, but the delay cost me some points.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me. I'd structure my whole week around what was due when. The LMS became my command center as deadlines approached—I'd be on there constantly checking requirements, submitting work, reviewing rubrics. The gamified elements helped too because they created mini-deadlines that kept me engaged even between major due dates. Without deadlines, I'd probably drift.
Q17: Feedback was really important to me. When I got good feedback, it boosted my confidence and made me want to engage more. Critical feedback initially spiked my anxiety, but I've learned to use it constructively. I'd often log back into the LMS specifically to check for feedback after submissions. It shaped how I approached future assignments—I'd adjust my strategies based on what instructors noted.
Q18: The course structure helped a lot because it was predictable. I knew when things were due, what was expected, and how different components connected. The gamified tasks added an extra layer of structure that kept me checking in regularly. Having clear modules and organized content made it easier to plan my 32 hours of study time. When structure was clear, my anxiety was more manageable.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier—they gave me concrete goals and rewards that kept me motivated. Clear navigation and well-organized content helped too. What made it harder was when instructions were vague or when too many things were due at once. Also, some technical glitches occasionally threw me off. Overall though, the design supported my learning style pretty well.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or confused and keeps logging in hoping things will click, but they're not ready to commit to submissions. Maybe they're doing a lot of exploring but struggling to produce finished work. It could also be perfectionism—they're engaging with everything but nothing feels good enough to submit. I've felt that way sometimes, where I'm active but paralyzed about output.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. A lot of my learning happened offline—reading printed notes, discussing with peers, working through problems on paper. Someone could have low LMS activity but be deeply engaged with the actual material. The LMS is just one tool; it doesn't capture everything. If you're efficient and know what you need, you might not need to log in constantly to do well.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long you spend on pages. They're good at tracking completion and frequency. But they miss the quality of engagement, whether you're actually thinking or just scrolling. They can't see offline studying, peer discussions, or the mental effort you're putting in. They definitely can't measure anxiety levels or how hard you're fighting to stay engaged.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm genuinely trying to understand and grow, not just checking boxes. It's when I'm curious about the material, asking questions, making connections to other things I know. It's showing up mentally, not just logging in. For me, engagement also means pushing through anxiety and staying committed even when it's hard. It's an active investment in my own learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied offline with printed materials and handwritten notes. It wouldn't capture my conversations with peers about course concepts or the YouTube videos I watched for extra clarification. It definitely wouldn't show my anxiety levels or the mental effort required to push through difficult moments. And it wouldn't reflect how the gamified tasks genuinely shifted my motivation—that internal change doesn't show up in clicks.
